planting.note: Plant dormant canes or containerized transplants in late winter through early spring; in zone 9 February–March planting gives canes the longest establishment season. Space 3–4 feet apart in rows 8–10 feet apart. Fruiting occurs in the second year from planting, typically May–June in zones 8–9.

directions: Plant dormant canes 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil (pH 6.0–6.5); Natchez is a semi-erect, thornless blackberry developed by the University of Arkansas and released in 2007, widely considered one of the finest named varieties for the Gulf Coast and mid-South. It is the earliest-ripening major thornless variety, typically producing its first berries in late May to early June in zones 8–9 — a full 2–3 weeks before Ouachita and other Arkansas releases. Berries are very large (9–12 grams), elongated, jet-black, sweet, and aromatic with excellent fresh-eating quality. Yields are high, and the self-supporting semi-erect canes need only minimal trellising. Prune out fruited floricanes after harvest and select 4–6 vigorous new primocanes per plant for next year's crop. Natchez is susceptible to double-blossom rosette disease in areas with high disease pressure; avoid overhead irrigation. In zone 9a, provide afternoon shade or plant on a north-facing slope to reduce heat stress during fruiting.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture Fruit Breeding — Natchez release notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Blackberry Variety Trials

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter through early spring; in zone 9 plant February–March for maximum establishment. Ouachita sets fruit in its second year, typically June in zone 8–9, slightly later than Natchez.

directions: Plant dormant canes 1–2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained acidic soil; Ouachita is a thornless, semi-erect blackberry developed by the University of Arkansas and released in 2003 — one of the most widely planted commercial blackberries in the mid-South and Gulf Coast because of its outstanding combination of productivity, fruit quality, and disease resistance. Berries are medium-large (7–9 grams), glossy black, firm, and mildly sweet with a pleasant balanced flavor and small seeds. Yields are among the highest of any named blackberry variety, and the plants show good resistance to orange rust and anthracnose — a meaningful advantage in the humid South. Canes are self-supporting but benefit from a simple T-trellis for row plantings. Prune floricanes to the ground immediately after harvest; thin primocanes to 4–6 per plant and tip at 36 inches in late summer to encourage lateral branching that will carry next year's fruit. Ouachita's mid-season ripening (about 2 weeks after Natchez) makes it an excellent partner variety for an extended harvest window.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Ouachita Blackberry release notes 2003
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Blackberry Variety Selection
LSU AgCenter Publication #2207 — Blackberry and Raspberry Production
Stark Bros Nursery blackberry variety notes

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or rooted transplants January–March in zone 9; Brazos is specifically adapted to Texas and Gulf Coast soils and climate, including alkaline, calcareous soils where many varieties struggle. Space 4–5 feet apart to accommodate vigorous growth.

directions: Plant dormant canes in well-drained soil in full sun; Brazos is a thorned, semi-erect blackberry developed by Texas A&M University and released in 1959 — for many decades it was the dominant commercial blackberry variety in Texas and the Gulf Coast South. Though newer thornless varieties have largely replaced it commercially, Brazos remains prized by home gardeners and farmers' market growers for its exceptional heat adaptation, flavor, and productivity on the alkaline soils that characterize much of central and south Texas. Berries are large, elongated, and very flavorful, ripening from late April through June in zone 9 — among the earliest major varieties. Unlike the University of Arkansas thornless releases (Natchez, Ouachita), Brazos tolerates alkaline soil pH (7.0–7.5) and calcareous caliche significantly better, making it the variety of choice for gardens on tight, rocky Hill Country soils or coastal prairie clay. Canes require trellising due to vigorous, semi-trailing growth. Wear heavy gloves when pruning. Remove fruited canes after harvest and select 4–5 of the strongest new canes per plant.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Blackberry Production in Texas (E-409)
Joe C. Brittain and B.D. Bruton, Texas Agricultural Experiment Station, Brazos blackberry release 1959
Texas Cooperative Extension — Small Fruit Production

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter through early spring; in zone 9 plant February–March. Kiowa is thorned, so wear heavy gloves during planting and all subsequent pruning. Space 4 feet apart in rows.

directions: Plant dormant canes 1–2 inches deep in fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; Kiowa is a thorned, erect blackberry developed by the University of Arkansas and released in 1996 as part of the same breeding program that produced Natchez and Ouachita. It is distinguished principally by its extraordinary berry size — among the largest of any blackberry variety, often exceeding 10–14 grams per fruit — and its exceptionally long harvest season that can extend 6–8 weeks through June and July in zone 8–9. Flavor is sweet and complex. The upright, thorned canes are self-supporting and vigorous, producing heavily. Kiowa shows good adaptability to the South and mid-South, performing well in humid, hot climates where some varieties decline. The principal trade-off is the thorns — unavoidable when harvesting and pruning — but many growers consider this worthwhile for the combination of size, yield, and long season. Prune fruited floricanes to ground level after the last berries are harvested; tip primocanes at 36–42 inches in summer to promote lateral branching.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Kiowa Blackberry release notes 1996
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Blackberry Varieties for Texas
North Carolina State Extension — Blackberry Production Guide
Journal of the American Society for Horticultural Science — Arkansas blackberry breeding program
